items.title: House price model on test set
items.scenario: A linear model predicts home prices in thousands of dollars. On 200 held-out homes, test MAE = $42k means the typical absolute miss is $42k. Test RMSE = $58k is higher because a few mansions with $200k+ errors inflate the squared-error average.
items.explanation: MAE answers "how far off are we on a typical home?" RMSE answers the same in dollars but weights catastrophic misses more — common when reporting to stakeholders after MSE training.

items.title: Temperature forecast comparison
items.scenario: Model A: test MAE = 1.2°C, test RMSE = 1.8°C. Model B: test MAE = 1.3°C, test RMSE = 1.5°C. B has slightly worse typical error (MAE) but fewer large misses (lower RMSE).
items.explanation: RMSE and MAE can disagree on which model is "better" — the choice depends on whether occasional large errors matter more than average accuracy.

items.title: Training MSE vs reporting RMSE
items.scenario: A team trains a demand forecaster minimizing MSE on the training set. For the quarterly report they publish test RMSE = 1,240 units — computed as √(test MSE), not the raw training number.
items.explanation: Same error family, different stage: MSE is optimized during fit (Loss Functions); RMSE is the scaled metric on unseen data for interpretability.

items.title: Baseline sanity check
items.scenario: Always predicting the training-set mean ŷ = ȳ gives test RMSE equal to the standard deviation of test y (when errors are roughly centered). A model with test RMSE worse than that baseline is underperforming a constant predictor.
items.explanation: RMSE on a mean baseline ties to spread of y (Expectation & Variance). Compare model metrics to simple baselines before trusting small improvements.
